name: GPT-5
archDisplay: Unified reasoning/non-reasoning system behind a real-time auto-router; dense transformer, exact size undisclosed
note: Unified model merging o-series reasoning with fast GPT responses via an auto-router.

results.notes: AIME 2025, no tools, high reasoning effort
GPQA Diamond, no tools, high reasoning effort
Humanity's Last Exam, no tools, high reasoning effort

SimpleQA (no web) accuracy for gpt-5-thinking = 55% (hallucination rate 40%), directly read from Table 8 of the official GPT-5 System Card PDF. gpt-5-thinking is the same reasoning-mode configuration already used for this model's existing hle=24.8 row (which the system card's own Figure 2 independently reconfirms as 24.8% for gpt-5-thinking), so the mapping to this corpus entry is consistent.

MMMU-Pro, GPT-5 with thinking/high reasoning effort = 78.4%, relayed from OpenAI's own launch-blog figures via Runbear, whose 'GPT-5 (thinking)' column was cross-checked and found to match this corpus's existing SWE-bench (74.9), AIME (94.6 implied via other quotes), HLE (24.8) and tau2-bench-telecom (96.7) rows exactly -- strong evidence Runbear is quoting OpenAI's self-reported figures verbatim rather than running its own eval.
Princeton HAL 'HAL Generalist Agent' scaffold, GPT-5 at 'Medium' reasoning effort (Aug 2025 snapshot). GAIA validation set.

ARC-AGI-1 semi-private evaluation set, run and verified directly by ARC Prize (not self-reported by OpenAI). Posted by ARC Prize's official account on launch day: 'GPT-5 on ARC-AGI Semi Private Eval: GPT-5 * ARC-AGI-1: 65.7%, $0.51/task'. Cross-confirmed via a second independent search summary citing the same figures. Not previously tracked for base GPT-5 (an arc-agi-1/2 pair already exists for GPT-5 Pro but not for this model).

ARC-AGI-2 semi-private evaluation set, run and verified by ARC Prize. Same official post as the ARC-AGI-1 entry: 'ARC-AGI-2: 9.9%, $0.73/task' for GPT-5 (distinct from GPT-5 Mini's 4.4% and GPT-5 Nano's 2.5% listed in the same post, and distinct from GPT-5 Pro's already-tracked 18.3% Epoch AI figure).

FrontierMath Tiers 1-3, Epoch AI's own scaffold, GPT-5 with high reasoning effort. Epoch's own launch-day post: 'GPT-5 sets a new record on FrontierMath! On our scaffold, GPT-5 with high reasoning effort scores 24.8% (±2.5%) ... in tiers 1-3'. This is a coincidental numeric match with GPT-5's already-tracked HLE score (also 24.8) but is a distinct benchmark/eval; verified via the primary Epoch AI research account post. Not previously tracked for base GPT-5 (only tracked for GPT-5 Pro).

FrontierMath Tier 4, same Epoch AI post as the tier1-3 entry: 'and 8.3% (±4.0%) in tiers 1-3 and 4, respectively' for GPT-5 with high reasoning effort. Distinct from GPT-5 Pro's already-tracked 19.5% tier-4 figure.

verificationNotes:
Existence, release date (Aug 7 2025, confirmed via TechCrunch) and pricing/context ($1.25/$10 per 1M, 400K context) confirmed by live-fetching developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5. The primary blog (openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-for-developers/) itself returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch, so each of the 7 result rows was independently cross-checked via secondary sources (Vellum, digitalapplied.com, Runbear, and search-engine excerpts) that quote the OpenAI figures verbatim: SWE-bench 74.9, AIME 2025 94.6, GPQA(no tools) 85.7 (distinct from GPT-5 Pro's 88.4/89.4-with-tools figures, so no conflict), HLE 24.8 ('jumping from 6.3% to 24.8%' with thinking), MMMU 84.2, τ2-bench telecom 96.7, Aider polyglot 88 — all confirmed exactly, no changes.

[2026-07-16] Resolved 4 core-benchmark gaps (mmlu, math, humaneval, livecodebench). Checked OpenAI's own official materials first: the launch blog (already established in prior notes as unreachable directly but cross-verified via secondary quoting sources) and the official GPT-5 System Card PDF (cdn.openai.com/gpt-5-system-card.pdf, downloaded and full-text-extracted via pdftotext for a thorough search) contain zero mentions of HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, or MATH/MATH-500 in any form, and no plain-English MMLU score -- the System Card's only MMLU-adjacent content is Table 11 (§3.11 'Multilingual Performance'), a professionally-translated 13-language variant ('MMLU Language') that excludes English entirely and is a distinct benchmark from this corpus's 'mmlu' slug, so it was not used. Independent leaderboards were then checked directly (raw HTML fetched via curl, not just AI-summarized snippets, since JS-rendered leaderboard sites often hide the real numbers from simple fetches): Artificial Analysis (artificialanalysis.ai) has a live, human-readable MATH-500 leaderboard confirming GPT-5 (high) = 99.4% (added). For MMLU, Artificial Analysis's full per-model dataset (verified across three separately-fetched pages) contains no plain 'mmlu' field for any model, only 'mmlu_pro' -- confirming the industry, including independent evaluators, has moved past plain MMLU as saturated/legacy, same as OpenAI. For HumanEval, Artificial Analysis no longer hosts a public leaderboard page for it at all (artificialanalysis.ai/evaluations/humaneval returns HTTP 404); it is not linked from GPT-5's current model page and displays nowhere as visible text on the site, so no citable claim exists even though a legacy value sits unexposed in AA's backend schema. For LiveCodeBench, Vals.ai's leaderboard (vals.ai/benchmarks/lcb) lists 'GPT 5 Mini' and 'GPT 5.2 Codex' but no plain 'GPT-5' entry, and Artificial Analysis's public LiveCodeBench page's visible leader-callout names Gemini 3 Pro Preview (91.7%) with GPT-5 (high) absent from any rendered text (it is flagged deprecated/superseded-by-GPT-5.1 in AA's system and excluded from the page's visible top-N comparisons) -- again, a legacy value exists only in unexposed backend JSON, not as a citable public claim. Both are therefore correctly omitted rather than guessed; this reflects genuine non-publication of these two legacy coding benchmarks for GPT-5 by both OpenAI and the independent trackers checked, not a research gap.

[2026-07-17] Second-pass deep research on the two remaining core-benchmark gaps (mmlu, humaneval), deliberately using source types NOT touched by the prior pass (which had exhausted official launch materials, System Card PDF, Epoch AI, Vals.ai, LMArena, BenchLM, llm-stats.com). Checked all 6 newly-assigned source types:

1. Stanford HELM -- fetched the live backend data directly (not just the JS-rendered frontend) for every HELM project: HELM Classic (v0.4.0, 142 models via classic_schema.json) and HELM Lite (v1.13.0, 91 models) contain zero GPT-5 entries at all (last model generation covered is GPT-4o/GPT-4 Turbo era). HELM Instruct (v1.0.0, 4 models) also has no GPT-5. HELM Capabilities (v1.15.0, crfm.stanford.edu/helm/capabilities/latest/) DOES include 'GPT-5 (2025-08-07)' (confirmed exact release-date match to this corpus entry) plus GPT-5 mini/nano/5.1 -- but its groups.json shows the entire scenario set for this suite is only 5 scenarios: MMLU-Pro, GPQA, IFEval, WildBench, and Omni-MATH (fetched core_scenarios.json and confirmed the header row directly). There is no plain-MMLU scenario and no HumanEval scenario anywhere in HELM Capabilities, and GPT-5 does not appear at all in the two HELM projects (Classic, Lite) that do run plain MMLU/HumanEval. Ruled out with certainty (not an access failure -- the actual scenario list was read).

2. arXiv / Semantic Scholar -- Semantic Scholar's public API returned HTTP 429 (rate-limited) on repeated attempts, so this leg leaned on arXiv full-text search instead. Found several papers mentioning GPT-5: 'Capabilities of GPT-5 on Multimodal Medical Reasoning' (2508.08224) and 'Evaluating GPT-5 as a Multimodal Clinical Reasoner' report only MMLU *medical-subdomain* subsets (not the full 57-subject MMLU this corpus's 'mmlu' slug requires) -- correctly not used, consistent with this corpus's variant-conflation policy. 'The Growing Pains of Frontier Models' (arXiv 2605.18840) has a 34-model comparison table but it is restricted to SWE-bench and GPQA Diamond only (confirmed via search snippet quoting GPT-5 SWE=74.9/GPQA=85.7, which just reconfirms values already in this corpus, no MMLU/HumanEval columns). GLM-5's arXiv report and Idea2Plan (2510.24891) mention GPT-5 only in the 'GPT-5.2 (xhigh)' or general-mention sense with no plain MMLU/HumanEval numeric table entry for base GPT-5. No arXiv paper found with a citable plain MMLU or HumanEval number for base GPT-5.

3. GitHub eval-harness run logs -- searched GitHub directly; found and fetched the raw committed data file (_data/model.yml) from github.com/swallow-llm/leaderboard (an academic Japanese-LLM eval-harness project that evaluates closed API models too). It has a real, dated entry `id: gpt-5-2025-08-07` (exact match to this corpus's release date) with a full results block: en_post = {HellaSwag, MMLU-Pro, GPQA, MATH500, AIME, LCB} and ja_post = {JamC-QA, WMT20, MIFEvalJa, MMLU-ProX, GPQA, PolyMathJaHT, JHumanEval}. Confirmed by reading the raw YAML directly: no plain 'MMLU' field and no plain English 'HumanEval' field exist for this model -- only MMLU-Pro/MMLU-ProX and a Japanese-translated JHumanEval variant, which per this corpus's own variant-conflation guardrail must not be used as substitutes for the 'mmlu'/'humaneval' slugs. No other public GitHub lm-evaluation-harness results.json for a GPT-5 API run was found.

4. Other leaderboards/aggregators -- OpenRouter's GPT-5 model page (openrouter.ai/openai/gpt-5) was fetched raw (curl, not just AI-summarized) and contains zero academic-benchmark fields of any kind (it only surfaces provider/uptime/throughput data, no MMLU/HumanEval). Scale AI SEAL leaderboards were confirmed (via their own category pages) to test only Coding (private SEAL set, not HumanEval), Math (GSM1k, not MMLU), Instruction Following, Multilingual, and Humanity's Last Exam -- no plain MMLU or HumanEval category exists on SEAL at all. LiveBench.ai's already-cached full CSV export has no plain 'gpt-5' row (only versioned gpt-5.2/5.4/5.5 variants), and LiveBench's own category set (math/coding/reasoning/language/data-analysis/instruction-following) doesn't include plain MMLU or HumanEval anyway. PapersWithCode's MMLU and HumanEval SOTA leaderboard URLs (paperswithcode.com/sota/...) now 302-redirect to huggingface.co/papers/trending -- the site is defunct/repurposed and no longer hosts these leaderboards. livecodebench.github.io's leaderboard is JS-rendered and could not be read directly; regardless, LiveCodeBench is a distinct benchmark from HumanEval (different problem set/methodology) so even a found score there would not be usable for the 'humaneval' slug.

5. Non-English-language sources -- the Swallow LLM Leaderboard check above doubles as this category (Japanese academic project, Tokyo Institute of Technology-affiliated). Also ran targeted Chinese (Zhihu) and Japanese-language searches for independent GPT-5 MMLU/HumanEval blog write-ups; results surfaced only comparisons of later snapshots (GPT-5.4, GPT-5.5, GLM-5 vs GPT-4o) or generic benchmark-explainer posts, no independent MMLU/HumanEval number for base GPT-5 (gpt-5-2025-08-07) specifically.

6. Sibling models -- checked whether any other model's comparison table carries GPT-5's plain MMLU/HumanEval score as a reference point: Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.5/4.7/4.8 and Sonnet 4.6 system cards (full text already cached from other research passes) mention 'MMLU' only in multilingual-methodology sections (Global MMLU / GMMLU citations) with no GPT-5 comparison row, and contain zero HumanEval mentions. GLM-5's arXiv report's comparison figures use 'GPT-5.2 (xhigh)', not base GPT-5. OpenAI's own later-model pages for GPT-5.1/5.4/5.5 (via benchmarklist.com mirrors, already cached) show 'MMLU Pro' comparison entries for those newer siblings, never plain MMLU, and never any HumanEval field, and never a base-GPT-5 reference row. No sibling-model table found carrying a plain MMLU or HumanEval score for base GPT-5.

Conclusion: across all 6 new source types, MMLU and HumanEval remain genuinely unpublished for base GPT-5 by every independent, academic, or aggregator source checked (in addition to the vendor materials the first pass already exhausted). This is consistent with the first pass's finding that OpenAI itself never published plain MMLU or HumanEval for GPT-5 (both are treated industry-wide as saturated/superseded by MMLU-Pro and LiveCodeBench respectively) -- both gaps are recorded as omitted_no_evidence rather than filled with an invented or mismatched-variant number.
